mnemonics.title: NoSQL Types Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: Remember NoSQL types with: KDCG - Key-Value, Document, Column-family, Graph. Think: 'Kids Don't Eat Carrots, Give Grapes' - silly but memorable!

mnemonics.title: ACID Properties
mnemonics.content: Atomic: All or Nothing | Consistency: Valid to Valid | Isolation: Independent | Durability: Persists after crash. Remember: 'A City Is Divided' or 'Always Consistency In Database'

mnemonics.title: CAP Theorem
mnemonics.content: CAP: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance. Can only pick 2 of 3. Remember: 'Choose A Pair' - pick any pair of the three!

mnemonics.title: Cypher Query Order
mnemonics.content: MATCH-WHERE-RETURN-ORDER BY-SKIP-LIMIT - Follow this sequence when writing Cypher queries. Remember: 'Match Where Return Orders Should Keep Learning'

acronyms.usage: Term for non-relational databases that don't use traditional SQL
Theorem describing trade-offs in distributed systems

acronyms.expansion: Not an acronym - named after cipher (code)

visualTips.visualization: Think of nodes as circles with labels (like :Person) written inside, and properties as tags attached to the circle. Each node can have multiple labels like a person can be both :Employee and :Customer.

Relationships are directed arrows with labels. The arrow shows direction: (A)->(B) means A knows B. Double-headed arrow means can traverse both ways. The relationship type (:KNOWS, :FRIEND) sits on the arrow.

Draw nodes as circles, relationships as labeled arrows. Properties go inside nodes or on arrows in curly braces {}. Think of it like a social network diagram where people are nodes and connections are arrows with details like 'since: 2020'.
Cypher ASCII art: () = node, -[]- = relationship, -> or <- = direction, {} = properties. (a:Person)-[:KNOWS]->(b:Person) reads exactly like a sentence: Person a knows Person b.

analogies.analogy: Graph databases are like GPS navigation - directly following roads (relationships) from point A to B. Relational databases are like searching a phone book index - good for finding one item, but takes multiple steps to trace connections.

Think of nodes as people at a party and relationships as handshakes or conversations. Each person (node) has attributes (name, age) and they connect to others through interactions (relationships) which can also have details (when, where, about what).
NoSQL databases are like a flexible workspace where you can rearrange furniture (schema) anytime. Traditional databases are like a fixed office layout - everything has a predetermined place.
It's like having a direct phone line to your best friend versus having to call directory assistance every time. Direct pointers mean instant connection, no lookup needed.
